24.tip: Double consonants always split: carro = car-ro, not carro.

24.title: Hiatus in Middle/Initial Positions
24.body: Vowels in hiatus (separate sounds) are divided when in initial or middle positions.
24.tip: Divide between vowels that don't form a diphthong, except at word ends.

24.title: Nasal Tilde (Ã, Õ)
24.body: The tilde (~) on Ã and Õ indicates nasalization and primary stress if no other accent is present.
24.tip: Ã and Õ are always nasal and stressed if unmarked by another accent.

24.title: Trema (Dieresis) Usage
24.body: The trema (¨) indicates that an unstressed U is pronounced after G or Q and before E or I.
24.tip: Trema tells you to say the 'u' in gu/qu + e/i combos.

24.title: Digraphs (CH, LH, NH)
24.body: Digraphs are two-letter combinations that represent a single consonant sound.
24.tip: CH, LH, NH are single sounds: CH as in 'chat', LH as 'lli' in 'million', NH as 'ny' in 'canyon'.

24.title: Adjectives with -ável/-ível
24.body: Portuguese forms adjectives meaning '-able' or '-ible' using suffixes -ável (after -ar verbs) and -ível (after -er/-ir verbs).
24.tip: -ável for -ar verbs, -ível for -er/-ir verbs. Add 'in-' for negatives: inevitável.

25.title: Possession with 'de'
25.body: Use 'de' (of) to show possession, similar to 's in English. Combine with articles to form contractions.
25.tip: Think 'the car of Paul' instead of 'Paul's car' to get the word order right.

25.title: Contractions: do, da, dos, das
25.body: 'De' contracts with definite articles: de + o = do, de + a = da, de + os = dos, de + as = das.
25.tip: These contractions are mandatory and very common in spoken Portuguese.

25.title: Location with 'ser' (permanent)
25.body: Use 'ser' for permanent or inherent locations, like cities, countries, or fixed addresses.

examples.en: The company is in the city.

25.tip: Use 'ser' for locations that don't change, like where something is established.

25.title: Location with 'estar' (temporary)
25.body: Use 'estar' for temporary or current locations, where someone or something is at the moment.
25.tip: Use 'estar' for locations that can change, like where someone is right now.

25.title: Time of day with 'ser'
25.body: Use 'ser' to tell time, including specific hours and expressions like noon and midnight.
25.tip: For telling time, always use 'ser' (é for singular, são for plural hours).

25.title: Physical/Emotional States with 'estar'
25.body: Use 'estar' to describe temporary physical conditions, emotions, or states of being.
25.tip: If it's a temporary condition (sick, tired, hungry), use 'estar'.

25.title: Present Progressive with 'estar'
25.body: Use 'estar' + gerund (-ndo) to describe actions happening right now (present continuous).
25.tip: This is like English '-ing' form. Estar + verb ending in -ndo.

25.title: Weather Expressions with 'estar'
25.body: Use 'estar' to describe current weather conditions, often in impersonal constructions.
25.tip: Weather is temporary, so use 'estar'. 'Fazendo' here means 'making' (it's making heat).

25.title: Possessive Pronouns: dele, dela
25.body: Use 'dele' (his) and 'dela' (hers) after the noun to show possession, instead of before.
25.tip: Think 'the car of him' = o carro dele. It always comes after the noun.

25.title: Contractions: ao, à
25.body: 'A' (to/at) contracts with definite articles: a + o = ao, a + a = à. Used with movement or location.
25.tip: These are common in directions and destinations. 'Ao' is masculine, 'à' is feminine.

25.title: Emphatic Construction: é que
25.body: Add 'é que' after a subject for emphasis, similar to 'it is... that...' in English.

examples.en: You are the one who has.
My boss is the one who knows.

25.tip: Use to stress who is doing the action. It doesn't change the meaning, just adds emphasis.

25.title: Distance: perto vs. longe
25.body: Use 'perto' for near and 'longe' for far. Often used with 'ser' for permanent distance.
25.tip: For fixed distances (like between cities), use 'ser'. For temporary closeness, use 'estar'.

26.title: Será que...? for 'I wonder if...'
26.body: Use 'Será que...?' to express 'I wonder if...' or 'Could it be that...?' It uses the future tense form of 'ser'.

examples.pt: Será que nós perdemos a nossa bagagem?
examples.en: I wonder if we lost our luggage.

examples.pt: Será que a comida está pronta?
examples.en: I wonder if the food is ready.

26.tip: Think of 'Será que...?' as a handy phrase to soften a question into a speculation.

26.title: Ter que vs. Ter de
26.body: Both 'ter que' and 'ter de' mean 'to have to' and are interchangeable before an infinitive.
26.tip: Remember: 'que' or 'de' after 'ter'—both lead to the action you must do.

26.title: Para + Infinitive for Purpose
26.body: Use 'para' followed by an infinitive to express 'to' do something, indicating purpose.

examples.pt: Eu tenho uma estória para contar.
examples.en: I have a story to tell.

26.tip: Link 'para' with an action to show what something is intended for.

26.title: Ouvir/Ver with Personal Infinitive
26.body: Verbs like 'ouvir' (to hear) and 'ver' (to see) can be followed by a personal infinitive to show perception of an action.
26.tip: After hearing or seeing, use the infinitive that agrees with who did the action.

26.title: Diminutive Endings -inho/-inha
26.body: Add -inho or -inha to nouns/adjectives to imply smallness, affection, or cuteness. Use cautiously as they are colloquial.

examples.en: little coffee (often a small, strong coffee)

26.tip: Attach -inho/-inha to the end of a word, but sometimes insert it before a final 's', like in 'Carlinhos'.

26.title: Café as Breakfast
26.body: In Brazil, 'café' can mean a full breakfast including coffee, rolls, fruit, and more, especially 'café completo'.
26.tip: Think of 'café' in the morning as more than just coffee—it's a meal.

26.title: Subir and Descer for Movement
26.body: Use 'subir' to mean 'to go up' and 'descer' to mean 'to go down', both for elevators, stairs, etc.

examples.en: I always go up in the elevator.

26.tip: Subir = ascend, Descer = descend. Use them for vertical movement.

26.title: Tem for Há (There is/are)
26.body: In spoken Brazilian Portuguese, 'tem' is often used instead of 'há' to mean 'there is' or 'there are'.
26.tip: In casual speech, 'tem' does double duty for 'has' and 'there is/are'.

26.title: O Senhor to O Seu
26.body: The formal title 'o senhor' is often shortened to 'o seu' before a name, as a polite form of address.
26.tip: Drop 'senhor' and use 'seu' with a name for a common respectful shortcut.

26.title: Numbers: Hundreds and Thousands
26.body: In Portuguese numbers, use 'e' after hundreds and a period (not comma) to separate thousands from hundreds.
26.tip: Think 'e' for 'and' after hundreds, and use a period like in many European number formats.

26.title: -ente/-ante Cognates
26.body: Many Portuguese words ending in -ente or -ante have similar English cognates, making them easier to learn.
26.tip: Spot -ente/-ante endings to guess meanings based on English words like 'student' or 'different'.

26.title: Carregador from Carregar
26.body: The noun 'carregador' (porter) is derived from the verb 'carregar' (to carry), showing a common word formation pattern.
26.tip: Add -dor to verb stems to form nouns for people who do that action, like 'carregar' to 'carregador'.

27.title: Imperfeito do Pretérito (describing past habits)
27.body: The imperfect past tense describes ongoing or habitual actions in the past, often translated as 'used to' or 'would'.

examples.pt: Ele viajava muito para o Brasil.
examples.en: He used to travel a lot to Brazil.

examples.pt: Ela sempre queria viajar pela Varig.
examples.en: She always wanted to travel with Varig.

27.tip: Use the imperfect for background descriptions and repeated past actions.

27.title: Pretérito Perfeito vs. Imperfeito (contrasting actions)
27.body: The perfect past (simple past) describes completed actions, while the imperfect describes ongoing or interrupted actions.

examples.pt: Eu queria chegar às nove, mas só cheguei às dez.
examples.en: I wanted to arrive at nine, but I only arrived at ten.

examples.pt: Eu ia falar com ele, mas ele saiu cedo.
examples.en: I was going to talk to him, but he left early.

27.tip: Perfect = what happened; Imperfect = what was happening/was going to happen.

27.title: Há + tempo (duration)
27.body: Use 'há' to indicate how long something has been happening, equivalent to 'for' in English.

examples.pt: Há quanto tempo o Jorge mora aqui?
examples.en: How long has Jorge lived here?

examples.pt: Eu estou aqui há dois anos.
examples.en: I have been here for two years.

27.tip: 'Há' + time period answers 'for how long?' questions.

27.title: Há... que construction
27.body: An alternative structure to express duration, starting the sentence with 'Há [time] que'.

examples.pt: Há dois anos que eu estou aqui.
examples.en: I have been here for two years.

examples.pt: Há um mês que eu conheço o Jorge.
examples.en: I have known Jorge for a month.

27.tip: This structure emphasizes the time period first: 'Há [time] que + verb'.

27.title: Numbers 20-100
27.body: Numbers from 20 to 100 follow consistent patterns with 'e' connecting tens and units.

examples.pt: vinte e um, trinta e dois, quarenta e cinco

examples.en: sixty, seventy, eighty, ninety, one hundred

27.tip: Use 'e' between tens and units (vinte e um), but not for multiples of ten alone.

27.title: Gender agreement with numbers
27.body: Numbers ending in 'um/uma' and 'dois/duas' must agree in gender with the noun they modify.

examples.pt: vinte e um meninos / vinte e uma meninas
trinta e dois meninos / trinta e duas meninas

27.tip: Only 'um/uma' and 'dois/duas' change for gender; other numbers remain the same.

27.title: Há with different time units
27.body: You can use 'há' with various time units like dias, semanas, meses, anos.

examples.pt: Há quantos dias o senhor mora aqui?
examples.en: For how many days have you lived here?

27.tip: Replace 'tempo' with specific time units: há quantos dias/meses/anos?

27.title: Casarse meaning 'to marry'
27.body: The verb 'casar' with the reflexive pronoun 'se' means 'to get married'.

examples.pt: O Santos gostava da Yara, mas casou com a Maria.
examples.en: Santos liked Yara, but he married Maria.

27.tip: Use 'casar com alguém' to say 'marry someone' - the preposition is important!

28.title: Tem for existence/availability
28.body: Use 'tem' to indicate existence or availability of something, often translated as 'there is/are'.
28.tip: Think of 'tem' as 'there is' - it's simpler than English which changes to 'there are' for plural.

28.title: Saber for knowledge/skills
28.body: Use 'saber' to express knowing facts or how to do something.

examples.en: She doesn't know how to swim.
Do you know how to drive?

28.tip: Saber = to know facts or skills, like 'saber' sounds like 'savvy' - having knowledge.

28.title: Mesmo as intensifier
28.body: Use 'mesmo' to intensify or emphasize, often meaning 'indeed' or 'really'.

examples.en: What (did you say) the name was?

28.tip: Mesmo adds emphasis - think 'me-SAME' stressing it's the same/very thing.

28.title: Falar que for reported speech
28.body: Use 'falar que' to report what someone said, similar to 'said that'.

examples.pt: O Marcos falou que trabalhava até às sete.
examples.en: Marcos said that he worked until seven.

examples.pt: Eu falei que eu era americano.
examples.en: I said that I was American.

28.tip: Falar que = 'say that' - introduces indirect speech with conjugated verb after.

28.title: Esquecer que for forgetting facts
28.body: Use 'esquecer que' when forgetting that something is true or happened.

examples.pt: Eu esqueci que a diária incluía o café.
examples.en: I forgot that the rate included breakfast.

examples.pt: Eu esqueci que o feriado era em fevereiro.
examples.en: I forgot that the holiday was in February.

28.tip: Esquecer que = 'forget that' - followed by a clause about what you forgot.

28.title: Esquecer + question words
28.body: Use 'esquecer' with question words (como, quando, quem, onde, o que) for forgetting details.

examples.pt: Eu esqueci como ela se chamava.
examples.en: I forgot what her name was.

examples.pt: Eu esqueci onde estava o carro.
examples.en: I forgot where the car was.

examples.pt: Eu esqueci o que ele queria.

28.tip: Esquecer + question word = forgetting specific information (how, when, who, where, what).

28.title: O seguinte for introducing topics
28.body: Use 'o seguinte' to introduce or present something, meaning 'the following'.

examples.en: The problem is the following/Here's the problem.
The issue is the following/Here is the issue.

28.tip: É o seguinte = 'it's as follows' - a formal way to introduce explanations.

28.title: Contrasting habitual vs specific actions
28.body: Use 'sempre' + imperfect for habitual past, contrast with preterite for specific instances.

examples.pt: Ângela sempre me dizia tudo. Mas esta vez ela não me disse nada.
examples.en: Angela always told me everything. But this time she didn't tell me anything.

examples.pt: Antes Luís lhe trazia flores. Mas esta vez ele lhe trouxe bombons.
examples.en: Before Luis would bring her flowers. But this time he brought her chocolates.

28.tip: Sempre + imperfect = always did (habit), esta vez + preterite = this one time (specific).

28.title: Reported intentions vs reality
28.body: Use imperfect after 'dizer/falar que' for reported intentions, contrast with preterite for what actually happened.

examples.pt: Ângela falou que ia me entregar as chaves, mas não entregou.
examples.en: Angela said she would give me the keys, but she didn't.

examples.pt: Ela disse que ia emagrecer, mas engordou.
examples.en: She said she would lose weight, but she gained weight.

28.tip: Falou que + imperfect (ia fazer) = said would do, mas + preterite = but actually did/didn't.

29.title: Irregular Command Forms from I-form
29.body: Most verbs with irregular I-form present tense use the same irregular stem in the singular command form.
29.tip: If the 'I' form is irregular (like saio, faço, repito), the command will use that same irregular stem.

29.title: More Irregular Command Forms
29.body: Verbs like seguir, dormir, and trazer have irregular I-forms that carry over to their command forms.
29.tip: Remember sigo (seguir), durmo (dormir), and trago (trazer) to form their commands: siga, durma, traga.

29.title: Command Forms Are Not Just Commands
29.body: Command forms are used for invitations, requests, admonitions, or getting attention, not just forceful orders.
29.tip: Think of command forms as 'suggestion forms'—they're often softened with 'por favor'.

29.title: Plural Commands for -AR Verbs
29.body: For -ar verbs, the plural command form ends in -em, which is the same as the present tense ending for -er/-ir verbs.
29.tip: Swap: -ar verbs take -em for plural commands, while -er/-ir verbs take -am.

29.title: Spelling Conventions in Commands
29.body: Spelling changes in command forms preserve the original verb's sound, like 'qu' for 'k' or 'j' for soft 'g'.
29.tip: Use 'qu' before 'e' to keep the 'k' sound (ficar → fique), and 'j' before 'a' to keep the soft 'g' sound (dirigir → dirija).

29.title: Plural Commands for -ER/-IR Verbs
29.body: For -er and -ir verbs, the plural command form ends in -am, the same as the present tense ending for -ar verbs.
29.tip: Remember the swap: -er/-ir verbs use -am for plural commands, mirroring -ar verbs' present tense.

29.title: Softening Commands with 'Por Favor'
29.body: Adding 'por favor' to a command form makes it a polite request rather than an order.
29.tip: When in doubt, add 'por favor' to sound polite and turn a command into a request.

30.title: Verbo Dizer - Presente
30.body: O verbo 'dizer' é irregular no presente. A forma 'eu' é 'digo', enquanto 'ele/ela/você' é 'diz' e 'nós' é 'dizemos'.

examples.en: We say that we are going to travel.

30.tip: Lembre-se: 'eu digo' (I say) é diferente de 'ele diz' (he says).

30.title: Verbo Dizer - Imperativo
30.body: O imperativo de 'dizer' usa formas irregulares. 'Diga' (você) e 'digam' (vocês) para afirmativo, com 'não' antes para negativo.
30.tip: Para comandos negativos, use 'não' + a forma do subjuntivo: não diga, não digam.

30.title: Verbos em -AR - Presente (Nós)
30.body: Na forma 'nós' dos verbos regulares em -AR, a vogal temática não é acentuada e permanece como 'e' fechado.

examples.en: We wake up at eight o'clock.
We load everything in the car.

30.tip: A terminação '-amos' tem o som de 'e' fechado, não aberto, em 'nós'.

30.title: Verbos com Mudança de Vogal E→E
30.body: Alguns verbos mudam o 'e' fechado da raiz para 'E' aberto em certas pessoas do presente, como 'conversar' e 'carregar'.

examples.en: João and Sérgio talk a lot.

30.tip: A vogal abre (E) quando a sílaba é tônica em 'ele/ela/você' e 'eles/elas/vocês'.

30.title: Verbos Úteis em -AR
30.body: Vários verbos regulares em -AR expressam ações comuns, como 'mostrar', 'trocar', 'melhorar' e 'engordar'.

examples.pt: Eu mostro a você onde fica São Paulo.
examples.en: I show you where São Paulo is.

examples.pt: João troca de carro todos os anos.
Quando você estuda, seu português melhora.
examples.en: When you study, your Portuguese improves.

30.tip: Estes verbos seguem a conjugação regular: -o, -a, -a, -amos, -am.

30.title: Verbo Engordar
30.body: O verbo 'engordar' significa ganhar peso. É regular em -AR e usado em contextos cotidianos.

examples.en: They gain a lot of weight in the winter.

examples.pt: Quando vamos ao Brasil engordamos muito.
examples.en: When we go to Brazil we gain a lot of weight.
She gained a lot of weight.

30.title: Pretérito Perfeito de Verbos em -AR
30.body: O pretérito perfeito de verbos regulares em -AR é formado com as terminações -ei, -ou, -ou, -amos, -aram.

examples.en: Did you reserve one for me?

30.tip: A terminação '-ou' é para 'ele/ela/você'. Ex: falou, chegou, comprou.

30.title: Verbos Irregulares no Pretérito
30.body: Alguns verbos têm formas irregulares no pretérito perfeito, como 'dizer' (disse), 'fazer' (fez), 'subir' (subiu).

examples.pt: O senhor não disse para a sua esposa?

examples.en: Have you already filled out the form?

30.tip: 'Subir' (to go up) e 'descer' (to go down) são irregulares: subiu, desceu.

30.title: Verbos com Dupla Consoante
30.body: Alguns verbos em -AR mantêm a consoante dupla em todas as formas, como 'telefonar' e 'entregar'.

examples.pt: O senhor não telefonou para a sua esposa?
Você entregou para o seu filho?
examples.en: Did you deliver it to your son?

30.tip: A grafia mantém 'nn' ou 'gg' para preservar o som forte da consoante.

30.title: Uso de 'que' com Verbos de Fala
30.body: Após verbos como 'dizer', use 'que' para introduzir uma oração subordinada com o que foi dito.

examples.en: He says that he can travel.
They say that they don't know.

30.tip: 'Que' funciona como 'that' em inglês em relatos indiretos.

30.title: Pronomes Demonstrativos Isto/Isso
30.body: 'Isto' refere-se a algo próximo do falante, 'isso' a algo próximo do ouvinte. Ambos são neutros em gênero.
30.tip: Pense: 'isto' = this here, 'isso' = that there (near you).

30.title: Expressões com Tudo/Nada
30.body: Use 'tudo' (everything) e 'nada' (nothing) com verbos para expressar totalidade ou ausência.
30.tip: Em frases negativas, 'nada' vem após o verbo, mas 'não' ainda é usado antes.
